nativeRange.habitat: Shallow to mid-depth Caribbean reefs, reef slopes, back-reef areas, and lagoon margins with moderate to strong water movement

decorPreferences: epoxy or gel-glue base attachment to rockwork
open water column positioning for flow exposure
adequate spacing from neighboring corals and anemones
avoid contact with substrate (branches resting on sand will rot)

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Has been reported to benefit from supplemental feeding two to three times per week with fine micro-plankton, phytoplankton, oyster eggs, Marine Snow, or similar coral foods. Supplementation with iodine, calcium, strontium, and trace elements is generally recommended.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS or SPS corals placed in direct contact
heavy algae/cyanobacteria-prone systems (algae can smother branches)

summary: The Slit-pore Gorgonian (Plexaurella spp.) is a Caribbean soft coral that harbors symbiotic algae for energy, giving it more tolerance than many non-photosynthetic gorgonians. Strong, turbulent flow is essential to keep the branches free of smothering algae.
careNotes:
Slit-pore Gorgonians are identified by club-tipped branches bearing the distinctive slit-shaped polyp openings that give the genus its common name. Under magnification, the skeleton contains 4-rayed 'butterfly' sclerites (tiny mineral rods) used for species identification. In the aquarium trade, Plexaurella is sometimes collected and shipped alongside Eunicea gorgonians and tends to be considered equally manageable — both are widely regarded as good choices for reef keepers ready to move beyond the easiest soft corals.

Plexaurella hosts symbiotic zooxanthellae (single-celled algae living inside the coral's tissue) and derives most of its energy from photosynthesis. Some online care references have described the genus as non-photosynthetic, which appears to reflect a confusion with related Caribbean gorgonians such as Diodogorgia or certain Muricea species that genuinely do not host zooxanthellae; trade and reef-aquaculture sources consistently report Plexaurella as photosynthetic.

Lighting: Moderate to moderately strong intensity is generally advised, around 120–200 PAR. Branch tips under very bright illumination have been reported to fail to open; in those cases, moving the colony to a slightly lower or partially shaded position can encourage polyp expansion. Middle-to-bottom tank placement is commonly recommended.

Flow: Strong, turbulent, non-laminar flow tends to be the single most critical husbandry factor. Wave-box or alternating-powerhead flow that mimics reef surge has been reported to produce the best polyp displays. Strong flow also serves as the primary defense against algae and cyanobacteria colonizing the exposed skeleton between the polyps.

Substrate contact: Branches must NOT be allowed to rest against sand or rockwork. Portions in contact with the substrate have been observed to suffocate, rot, turn black, and slough off the central axis. The colony should be anchored upright using reef epoxy or gel-glue at the base only, with all branches extending freely into the water column.

Feeding: Although photosynthetic, supplemental feeding two to three times per week with fine micro-plankton — phytoplankton, oyster eggs, Marine Snow, or baby brine shrimp — has been reported to improve polyp extension and overall growth rate.

Algae management: As with other gorgonians, the most common cause of failure in captivity is algae or cyanobacteria smothering the colony skeleton. Affected areas can be gently cleaned with a soft brush, and brief freshwater dips can be used for more severe outbreaks. Maintaining strong flow and stable, moderate-nutrient water quality substantially reduces this risk.

Branches are reported to be brittle and break easily during handling. The Slit-pore Gorgonian is generally considered reef-safe, with no sweeper tentacles or documented strong chemical aggression toward neighboring corals, though adequate spacing from other gorgonians and especially anemones is still advised. Wild colonies of P. nutans can reach approximately 130 cm; aquarium specimens typically arrive at 10–25 cm and have been reported to reach 30–60 cm over time under favorable conditions.
breedingNotes: On natural Caribbean reefs, Plexaurella reproduces sexually through broadcast spawning, releasing eggs and sperm into the water column. Deliberate captive sexual breeding is not commonly reported in the hobby. The primary propagation method is fragmentation: cutting a branch and reattaching its base to live rock with reef epoxy. This has been reported to succeed readily when fragments are of sufficient size and water quality remains stable.

primary.name: World Wide Corals – Plexaurella search
primary.notes: Primary source search returned no dedicated Plexaurella product page at time of access. Swapped to Pacific East Aquaculture (Giant Slit Pore Photosynthetic Gorgonian) as effective primary; documented here for source-chain traceability.

additional.name: Pacific East Aquaculture – Giant Slit Pore Photosynthetic Gorgonian
additional.notes: Used as effective primary after World Wide Corals had no dedicated page. Provided temperature 78-80°F (25.5-26.7°C), salinity 1.025-1.026 SG, dKH 7.5-8.5, calcium 420-450 ppm, magnesium 1250-1350 ppm, high lighting, moderate-to-strong flow, middle-to-upper placement, care level easy-to-moderate, photosynthetic status. Lists species as Plexaura flexuosa under the slit-pore-gorgonian umbrella — sometimes Plexaura and Plexaurella are conflated in the trade.

additional.name: tankfacts.com – Giant Caribbean Slit Pore Gorgonian (Plexaurella nutans)
additional.notes: Cross-checked high-stakes parameters: temperature 76-80°F (24.4-26.7°C), pH 7.8-8.4, salinity 1.023-1.026 SG, dKH 8-12, calcium 380-450 ppm, magnesium 1200-1400 ppm. Confirmed bottom-tank placement, strong flow requirement, brittle-branch handling caveat. Source classified species as 'non-photosynthetic,' which conflicts with majority of sources — averaged kH bound and adopted the broader 8-12 dKH range.

additional.name: ReefGuide.org – Slit-Pore Sea Rods (Plexaurella spp.)
additional.notes: Confirmed Caribbean/Bahamas/Florida native range, depth range 1-50 m, colony size 15-90 cm, family Plexauridae. Described as 'very hardy' and recommended as a 'beginner's coral' — adopted as the rationale for placing careLevel at intermediate rather than advanced.

additional.name: Oceans Garden – Eco-Friendly Giant Slit Pore Gorgonian (Plexaurella nutans)
additional.notes: Cross-checked photosynthetic status (confirmed zooxanthellate), moderate lighting, moderate flow, middle placement, peaceful temperament, reef-safe. Confirmed size 2-6 inches at typical sale; supports hardy/aquarium-friendly classification.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Plexaurella (taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only: Family Plexauridae, Order Malacalcyonacea, Class Octocorallia. Confirmed ~14 species in genus, characterized by slit-pores and 4-rayed butterfly sclerites. Not used for husbandry parameters.
